episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Community Tech & Governance
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Community Tech & Governance.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [BC Gov News] Cracking down on organized crime, gun violence and [BC Gov News] Dewdney Bridge replacement reaches completion, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: When rural communities adopt external technology solutions (commercial or government-funded), what governance structures must exist to ensure sustainable, community-controlled outcomes? Topics include: Commercial vs. community-owned infrastructure as a false binary disguising the real variable of governance capacity, Indigenous language technology sustainability: funding, technical maintenance, and data sovereignty in federally-funded projects like CILT, Regional coordination models for Indigenous technical services and the risk of pooled decision-making erasing smaller communities' urgencies, Operations budget and embedded technical training as necessary conditions for technology adoption in resource-constrained communities, Governance readiness standards for rural digital infrastructure decisions across municipalities and First Nations.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Williams Lake Association for Community Living

discussion.central_question: When rural communities adopt external technology solutions (commercial or government-funded), what governance structures must exist to ensure sustainable, community-controlled outcomes?
discussion.riley_position: Commercial hybrid solutions like T-Mobile's Starlink backup offer practical connectivity improvements that most rural communities couldn't achieve through independent infrastructure projects, but governance capacity—not technology ownership—is the determining factor in success.
riley_key_evidence: T-Mobile's hybrid connectivity model formalizes an architecture rural communities have improvised for years, with Starlink latency improvements making it genuinely viable for emergency communications backup
Olds, Alberta's MRNet fiber co-op took eight years and significant municipal capital, a runway most Cariboo communities don't have
Governance readiness checklist could be a practical tool for Indigenous and municipal decision-makers without requiring external consultants
discussion.casey_position: Adopting external technology without pre-existing governance infrastructure—including operations budgets, technical maintenance capacity, and data sovereignty protocols—transfers risk from institutions to communities and creates orphaned systems when external partners exit.
casey_key_evidence: Vancouver's lifeguard funding cuts transfer risk from public budgets to individuals and liability claims, mirroring how grant-dependent tech projects fail at year-five when maintenance costs weren't budgeted

Indigenous language technology projects require epistemological sovereignty over data that standard licensing frameworks don't address, and require embedded technical training—not just workshops—to prevent knowledge remaining external
Williams Lake Association for Community Living sustained community programming for 66 years through institutional durability rather than grant cycles
discussion.resolution: Riley conceded that technology optimism ('adopt early, iterate fast') fails without governance infrastructure, and both agreed that the core question before any technology adoption should be 'can we govern this five years from now without the external partner?'—shifting focus from technology choice to governance capacity design.
topics_covered: Commercial vs. community-owned infrastructure as a false binary disguising the real variable of governance capacity
Indigenous language technology sustainability: funding, technical maintenance, and data sovereignty in federally-funded projects like CILT
Regional coordination models for Indigenous technical services and the risk of pooled decision-making erasing smaller communities' urgencies
Operations budget and embedded technical training as necessary conditions for technology adoption in resource-constrained communities
Governance readiness standards for rural digital infrastructure decisions across municipalities and First Nations
